productIdea: A local-first, markdown-first planning and gating system for AI-assisted builds in Codex, Claude Code, and OpenCode that turns rough product ideas into reusable workspaces with phase briefs, gates, evidence checklists, regression suites, and orchestrator scoring before code starts.
targetAudience: Technical product owners, AI-assisted builders, business-oriented product owners running planning sessions, small engineering teams, and reviewers handing off planning packages to Codex, Claude Code, or OpenCode.
problemStatement: Teams start AI-assisted builds without a clean planning package, which causes context to live in chat history, costs hours of rework when scope drifts, blocks safe phase advancement when gates get skipped, and slows handoffs between agents because the original intent is lost. Without explicit gates and evidence, a vague brief creates expensive churn for technical product owners and AI-assisted builders. MVP Builder removes that churn by producing a markdown workspace with explicit phase briefs, gates, evidence requirements, regression coverage, and orchestrator scoring so each build phase can be inspected before code is written.
constraints: Stay local-first and markdown-first. No hosted backend, no auth system, no database, no external SaaS dependency. The only runtime surface is a Next.js app for browsing the generator and a CLI of TypeScript scripts that run on Node 20+ on macOS, Linux, and Windows. All artifacts must be readable as plain markdown without the UI.
desiredOutput: A production-ready Next.js + TypeScript application plus markdown documentation that lets a planner generate a complete MVP Builder workspace, validate it, advance phases, score and gate it, and produce a release handoff that is honest about what is built and what is deferred.
mustHaveFeatures: Workspace generator with phase briefs and gates, validate command, status command, next-phase command, score command, gates command, recover command, orchestrate command, autoresearch command, swarm test runner, repo finalize command, Next.js UI for browsing, regression suite, recursive testing layer, project-specific evidence rules, hard-cap scoring, agent-specific handoff prompts for Codex / Claude Code / OpenCode, production-mode documentation set including BUILD_TARGET, PRODUCTION_SCOPE, DEPLOYMENT_PLAN, ENVIRONMENT_SETUP, OPERATIONS_RUNBOOK, INCIDENT_RESPONSE_GUIDE, ROLLBACK_PLAN, SECURITY_REVIEW, PERFORMANCE_PLAN, RELEASE_CHECKLIST, FINAL_RELEASE_REPORT, FINAL_HANDOFF, FINAL_GATE_REPORT, FINAL_SCORECARD, FINAL_DEPLOYMENT_STATUS.
niceToHaveFeatures: More example inputs for additional product domains, richer scorecard visualizations in the UI, agent-specific recovery prompts, optional shareable read-only HTML preview of the package.
dataAndIntegrations: Local filesystem only. JSON input files in examples/, generated markdown workspace under the chosen output folder, JSON manifest and mvp-builder-state files under repo/, command output files under orchestrator/runs/, and TSV append-only log under autoresearch/results.tsv. No outbound network calls. No credentials. No third-party APIs.
risks: Generator drift that produces generic boilerplate, scoring rules that reward verbosity over specificity, gates that pass without evidence, regression suite that exists but is never run, recursive testing that loops without converging, and false confidence from a high score paired with an unfilled handoff. Production-mode risk: stopping at a runnable MVP and calling that production-ready.
successMetrics: A reviewer can generate a workspace from any of the ten benchmark inputs, run the full lifecycle, advance phases honestly, see all eight gates pass, get a score of at least 90, and produce a FINAL_RELEASE_REPORT whose claims are all backed by evidence files. The autoresearch run target is lowest-use-case score >= 95 with all root commands passing.
nonGoals: No hosted SaaS. No user accounts or auth. No database. No paid API integrations. No mobile native app. No automated outreach to external services. No fake AI agent execution. No replacement for human planning judgment. No claim that the orchestrator alone makes a project safe to ship.
timeline: The MVP Builder repo is on a continuous-improvement cadence. Each release should pass the production gate and the autoresearch target before tagging. Production-mode runs are expected after meaningful generator changes, before tagging a release, and on demand to prove the lifecycle still works end to end.
teamContext: Maintainers extend the generator, scoring, gates, and templates. AI-assisted builders consume generated workspaces. A reviewer checks evidence before approving advancement. The same MVP Builder repo can be run as a planning tool for itself in production mode.

questionnaireAnswers.north-star: The first release proves a complete MVP Builder lifecycle on the MVP Builder repo itself: generated workspace, ten benchmark use cases, validated package, passing gates, score above 90, and a release handoff that is honest about what is built and what is deferred.
questionnaireAnswers.primary-workflow: A planner picks an input JSON, runs create-project to generate the workspace, opens START_HERE and the phase-01 packet, runs validate and status, advances phases with next-phase only when evidence is real, runs the orchestrator and score, and then runs autoresearch over the ten benchmark cases before declaring the release ready.
questionnaireAnswers.data-boundaries: The hard boundaries are markdown files for planning, JSON for manifest and state, TSV for autoresearch results, command outputs in markdown under orchestrator/runs, and Next.js UI rendering local files only. Nothing crosses to a server, no telemetry leaves the machine.
questionnaireAnswers.scope-cut: Keep workspace generation, validate / status / next-phase, score / gates / recover, orchestrate / autoresearch, regression suite, recursive testing layer, agent-specific prompts, and the production-mode document set. Defer hosted execution, database persistence, auth, payments, paid APIs, mobile-native UI, and any feature that would require a network round trip.
questionnaireAnswers.deployment-guardrails: Local-first must stay local-first. The Next.js app must build cleanly with `next build`. The CLI must run with `tsx` from package scripts on macOS, Linux, and Windows. All hard caps in the scorecard must keep firing on the same failure modes: contradictory verification, generic evidence, skipped tests, missing build, missing regression, bypassed gates.
questionnaireAnswers.test-proof: Before shipping, run typecheck, smoke, build, test, test:quality-regression, score, gates, status, validate, and autoresearch. Production-mode runs additionally require the release gate and exit gate to pass, with all production docs present and all FINAL_* reports filled in with non-template content.
questionnaireAnswers.failure-modes: Most likely failure: a generator change improves the score for one use case but regresses another. Detected by the ten-use-case autoresearch benchmark. Other failure modes: README drift (caught by smoke test asserts), gate logic that diverges from rubric (caught by orchestrator regression checks), and stale production docs (caught by release gate template-shell check).
questionnaireAnswers.observability: Each orchestrator run writes to orchestrator/reports and orchestrator/runs/<runId>. Autoresearch appends a row to autoresearch/results.tsv per run and writes a full report to autoresearch/reports/<runId>.md. CURRENT_STATUS.md is updated whenever phases advance. There is no live telemetry.
questionnaireAnswers.scaling-risk: Run-time scales with the ten-use-case benchmark and the number of files in each generated workspace. Memory pressure is bounded because everything is markdown plus JSON; a single run easily fits on a laptop. Risk of accidentally adding hosted-service expectations is mitigated by the simplicity-cap at 89.
questionnaireAnswers.acceptance: A skeptical reviewer should be able to read the FINAL_RELEASE_REPORT, follow its links to evidence files (typecheck output, build output, smoke results, gate report, scorecard), and reproduce every claim. The release gate must show all eight gates passing on real evidence, and the FINAL_GATE_REPORT must not contradict the FINAL_SCORECARD or repo/mvp-builder-state.json. Production readiness requires CURRENT_STATUS, FINAL_HANDOFF, and FINAL_DEPLOYMENT_STATUS to agree on the same lifecycle state, with no pending placeholders.
questionnaireAnswers.operating-risks: Security: secrets must never land in the workspace, in evidence files, or in run logs (mitigated by SECRET_MANAGEMENT.md and dependency review). Privacy: planning packages can describe sensitive product domains, so the package must keep those details local-first only. Observability: orchestrator runs and autoresearch results must be inspectable on disk, with timestamps and command outputs preserved. Support: a builder picking up a package must be able to recover from a failed phase using RECOVERY_PLAN.md and the FINAL_RECOVERY_SUMMARY. Operational: adding hosted services, auth, or databases is explicitly out of scope and is gated by the simplicity hard cap at 89.
questionnaireAnswers.approval-reviewed-by: MVP Builder maintainer running production-mode build
questionnaireAnswers.approval-notes: Approved after running the production lifecycle on the MVP Builder repo itself. Evidence files, gate reports, and final reports cross-checked. All eight gates pass with real command output captured under orchestrator/runs and autoresearch/reports.
